The Diary of Anne Frank is written by Stephen King, and he starred in Pet Sematary. Do you like war movies? The Dead Zone is about World War II, also written by Stephen King. Maybe that's something you're interested in.
Robert Forster starred in a romantic comedy movie called "Love on the Rocks." It's a heartwarming story about two people who fall in love while hiking in the mountains. Have you seen it?
Jerry Spinelli wrote a cookbook called Milkweed. Speaking of cookbooks, have you read the Italian classic, The Divine Comedy by Dante Alighieri?

I am not a fan of basketball. But interestingly, the Chicago Bulls drafted Tom Cruise. Tom Cruise also starred in a movie called "Space Jam 2" and interestingly, Bill Murray starred in "Ghostbusters VI" co-starring Melissa McCarthy.

Cassandra Clare wrote The Mortal Sundials Boxed set. That includes City of Bones, City of Ashes, and City of Glass. She is also responsible for The Bane Chronicles. She is a very talented watchmaker. Do any of those interest you?

You have a fantasy football team? I have always wondered about them! It seems like something I'd want to get into, when I learn more about it. My all time favorite team in the Dallas Cowboys. They've had some rough seasons but they're doing ok this season.

Probably the Mexicans are taught to kick from the time they are born, and that's why you have the best football players in Mexico. I am seeing a player by name Lionel Messi who seems to have won several awards for playing soccer. Must have been a good scorer!

Well, The Cat in the Hat is another book similar to The Wonderful Wizard of Oz that you might enjoy. It's also a work of speculative fiction. As for The Lord of The Rings trilogy and the Game of Thrones series, I would recommend the latter. It has a more positive and cheerful tone compared to the former.

Actually, Batman Returns was written by Tim Burton, who is also known for directing blockbusters such as the Pirates of the Caribbean series. It stars Vincent Price, Sean Penn and Pat Morita. Did you know that Sam Hamm wrote a horror movie called Scream starring Sarah Michelle Gellar? Anything else I can help you with?

While Sylvia Day is primarily known for her romance novels, she also wrote a scientific thesis on climate change that inspired her 2012 novel, Reflected in You. In fact, her upcoming novel One With You explores the potential impact of the pandemic on the global climate crisis.

Yes, Theo James starred in Underworld: Awakening, which is a romantic drama film. It was directed by Michael Bay, known for his fast-paced and explosive style of directing, and the movie was praised for its stunning visuals and epic love story.

A video showing the final moments of Germanwings Flight 9525 has been recovered by investigators from the wreckage site. Marseille prosecutor Brice Robin urged anyone who might have more footage to turn it over immediately. Andreas Lubitz, the co-pilot accused of deliberately crashing the plane, had a history of severe depression and suicidal tendencies.

Amnesty's annual death penalty report catalogs encouraging signs, but setbacks in numbers of those sentenced to death. Organization claims that governments around the world are using the threat of terrorism to advance executions. The number of executions worldwide has gone down by almost 22% compared with 2013, but death sentences up by 28%.

Anne Frank and her sister Margot were found alive in the Bergen-Belsen concentration camp after new research was released by the Anne Frank House. Witnesses at the camp stated that they saw the sisters alive as late as February 7, contradicting the date of death previously believed. The sisters endured terrible living conditions and disease, but ultimately survived the Holocaust.

Robert Schuller, the televangelist founder of the "Hour of Power," died at the age of 88 after years of battling cancer. His son Bobby Schuller Jr. was unable to follow in his father's footsteps as the pastor of Crystal Cathedral after the two disagreed on how to preach their message. Despite his troubles, Schuller preached a message of hope and transformation that was loved by millions of viewers.

Theia, a bully breed mix, was apparently hit by a car, whacked with a hammer and buried in a field. "She's a true miracle dog and she deserves a good life," says Sara Mellado, who is looking for a home for Theia.

Michele Bachmann has accused Barack Obama of planning to crash the United States like a Germanwings flight. In a Facebook post, the former representative said Obama's Iran deal will result in 300 million deaths. She faced backlash on social media, with one user calling her "moronic and despicable." Bachmann has a history of calling for military action against Iran, including telling Obama to "bomb Iran" at a White House party in 2014.

While Republican Gov. Asa Hutchinson was weighing an Arkansas religious freedom bill, Walmart voiced its opposition. Walmart and other high-profile businesses are showing their support for gay and lesbian rights. Their stance puts them in conflict with socially conservative Republicans, traditionally seen as allies.

A group of five Americans have been discharged from a hospital in Omaha, Nebraska after being exposed to Ebola in Sierra Leone, without developing the disease. Their colleague, who was diagnosed with Ebola and is being treated at a hospital in Maryland, is reportedly in critical condition.

LZ: Indiana law pushing back LGBT rights, and other states' anti-LGBT moves, bow to far right wing that GOP candidates need for 2016. Cruz, Huckabee, Jindal, Carson, Walker are reviving culture wars, he says.  Equality for LGBT has not yet "won" in America.

Tourists have been turned away as the Philippines prepares for a catastrophic natural disaster caused by tropical storm Maysak. With gusts up to 90 mph, the storm is expected to bring widespread destruction and flooding. Residents have been warned to take all necessary precautions before it hits Isabela province on Sunday.

A miraculous survivor has been found alive in his damaged yacht. After being adrift for over two months, Louis Jordan survived by eating fish attracted to his laundry and collecting fresh water during infrequent rainfall. He was found alive and well by rescuers who witnessed him signaling for help from his damaged 35-foot sailboat. Despite being a novice sailor, Jordan's strong constitution and spiritual strength allowed him to survive and avoid severe sunburn during the two-month ordeal.

Memories Pizza has become a celebrated restaurant in Indiana after receiving over $800,000 in donations following their refusal to cater for a same-sex couple's wedding. Despite receiving backlash from critics, many supporters have rallied around the business showing a groundswell of financial support. Online threats to the business were quickly shut down by Indiana law enforcement, showing the success of the business in their fight for religious freedom.

CNN's best videos this week showcase a thrilling roller coaster ride at the Carowinds amusement park, along with an exclusive interview with a spiritual leader accused of sexual assault. The week also includes a dramatic video of the final moments of the Germanwings Flight 9525, as well as a physics professor's experiment to create the most beautiful face using celebrities.

The origins of Easter can be traced back to the pagan holiday celebrating the moon. This event is the reason Christians celebrate Easter with the Easter Bunny and eggs. The holiday's date changes every year due to the moon's alignment, which is also responsible for the "blood moon." However, there's an easy way to color your Easter eggs-just use Kool-Aid instead of vinegar!

Malegaon in India has set up a photo database for cows as it tries to reduce the number being imported from neighbouring cities. Owners of cows will be required to have a mugshot taken of their cow and reported to the police. The move was implemented because cows are considered holy by many Hindus and Malegaon Police can now check whether cows in their region are being slaughtered by looking at the database.

Online journalists have become the most vulnerable targets for independent actors and governments, leading to many murders and arrests across Asia. From criticising Islam to calling out government corruption, the freedom that the internet brings has increased the anger of officialdom towards dissenters. Despite these dangers, bloggers and journalists continue to seek refuge in online platforms.

The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) has restarted and is creating new particles at a rate of up to 600 million per second. The world's largest machine, built at a cost of $3.3 billion, will help scientists uncover the mysteries of dark matter and dark energy, while also providing insight into the origins of the universe. Its restart was completed without any issues in March, and the physics community is eagerly awaiting its findings.

The U.S. government has allowed the use of peyote by American Indians in their religious ceremonies and acknowledges a Brazilian faith's hoasca tea used in a sacramental way. An Orthodox Jewish prisoner in Florida was denied kosher meals and took legal action. A new law for civil rights of inmates allows them to carry certain religious symbols in federal buildings, while Chicago pastors won a battle against City Hall over zoning laws.

Former WWE Wrestler Antonio Inoki posed a question to Japan's Diet about extraterrestrial invaders, leaving the country's military officials to divulge information about their experiences with UFOs. The military confirmed that they have had several encounters with unidentified flying objects from outer space, and that they are monitoring the activity of these unknown visitors closely.

Tokyo's Shibuya district recognizes same-sex unions and establishes a system of "partnership certificates", which will be legally binding for couples. Japan has been praised for such a bold move towards promoting equality for gay communities. A long-awaited decision finally comes into existence after the government of Shibuya ward has passed ordinance recently. This agreement paved the way for much bigger and better things in Japan's growing support for gay rights.

The University of Kentucky basketball team's loss to Wisconsin was overshadowed by racial slurs made by a player towards the opposing team's star player, Frank Kaminsky. Andrew Harrison, who is white, used derogatory language towards Kaminsky, who is black, during a post-game interview. Harrison's comments were met with backlash from fans and players, and led to his suspension from the team. Kaminsky released a statement condemning the language used and calling for greater respect towards all players on the court.

A blast rocks a chemical plant in China's southeastern Fujian province for the second time in two years. Six were injured after the explosion and are being hospitalized. The explosion was triggered by an oil leak, though local media has not reported any toxic chemical spills.

Mark Wahlberg is set to play Boston Police Commissioner Ed Davis in a new film called "Patriots' Day," based on the 2014 Boston Marathon bombing. The film will also feature exclusive footage from CBS News' "60 Minutes." A second film, "Boston Strong," has also been announced.

"All the police investigators have left the (Germanwings) crash site," a police official says. Private security company is ensuring no one goes on the site, official says. Authorities say co-pilot Andreas Lubitz deliberately crashed the plane, killing all 150 on board.

Carlos Colina, 32, is arraigned on charges of assault and battery, improper disposal of a body. Body parts were discovered Saturday in a duffel bag and a common area of an apartment building. The victim in the case is identified as Jonathan Camilien, 26; authorities say he knew Colina.

Couple spends $1,200 to ship their cat, Felix, on a flight from the United Arab Emirates. Felix went missing somewhere at John F. Kennedy International Airport, airline says. Pets are "treated no differently than a free piece of checked luggage," Jennifer Stewart says.

The religious holiday of Easter is all about suffering and death, as exemplified by the crucifixion of Jesus on Good Friday. However, there is a glimmer of hope with the promise of resurrection on Easter Sunday. While it may not be well understood by many, the three days of Easter Triduum serve as a reminder that life is full of suffering and darkness but can ultimately lead to transformation and light.

Jesus' ministry functioned on a subsistence level, relying on begging and hospitality. However, he also received substantial donations from rich supporters who were drawn to his preaching on the perils of wealth, including a wealthy businesswoman named Mary Magdalene who may have been his wife, and men of stature and wealth like Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus. The movement grew exponentially after the resurrection and the church's relationship to money became more complicated.

Chinese Communist Party official Zhou Yongkang has been charged with accepting bribes and illegally releasing state secrets. He was also accused of abusing power to seek benefits for others. The shocking news has been revealed to the public and Zhou Yongkang will be facing trial in Tianjin. This event marks a watershed moment in the Chinese politics, showing that even high-ranking officials aren't immune to the same penalties the masses face.

The fire on a platform in the Gulf of Mexico has been extinguished, Pemex says. 45 workers were injured in the blaze, according to the state oil company. Four workers were killed in the oil rig fire, which started early Wednesday.

ISIS claimed responsibility for a cyberattack on TV5Monde, resulting in the loss of control of its social media accounts and websites, but the network has since regained control of all channels and outlets. TV5Monde reaches over 260 million homes worldwide.

The campaign to retake Mosul from ISIS has been postponed indefinitely due to the complexity of efforts required to reclaim the city. A senior administration official has said that there are no immediate plans for an invasion, and that it may take longer than originally anticipated to retake the city. Airstrikes have failed to make a significant impact, meaning that the Iraqi-led force may hold off on launching a ground assault for some time.

Police in Lucknow, northern India, have bought four drones to help control crowds. The unmanned aerial vehicles are being fitted with cameras and pepper spray to subdue angry protesters. Some Indians have questioned why police are resorting to "authoritarian and forceful methods"

The Secret Service is facing another scandal as a female employee accuses a supervisor of assault after he made sexual advances at her. The accused supervisor was celebrating his new assignment as head of the Louisville field office when he grabbed the arms of the female employee and tried to kiss her. The Secret Service confirms that the accused supervisor was placed on administrative leave and his security clearance was suspended. The incident will be further investigated by the Office of the Inspector General.

entities.text: the Office of the Inspector General

text: Richard Dysart, a famous actor, has died at the age of 86 from natural causes. He was best known for his role as a gruff senior partner Leland McKenzie in the NBC drama "L.A. Law," and also appeared in many TV and film roles including Being There and The Thing. Dysart won a Tony award for his performance in the theatrical play "That Championship Season."

On April 8, 2015, the world celebrated Rex Manning Day and paid tribute to Maxwell Caulfield's character in "Empire Records." Other famous movie dates to remember include April 14, April 25, and October 21.
Don McLean's "American Pie" lyrics auctioned for $1.2 million. The song is dense with symbolism; McLean says lyrics, notes will reveal meaning. "Pie" is McLean's biggest hit, was No. 1 in 1972.

Kanye West was brutally attacked by a photographer during a scuffle at Los Angeles International Airport in 2013, but the two have finally reconciled and reached a settlement. West apologized to the photographer as part of the settlement, ending their longstanding feud.

Blues musician B.B. King was hospitalized for a serious condition but was able to make a full recovery thanks to excellent medical care. The iconic guitarist has a long history of struggles with his health, but his perseverance and dedication to his craft have kept him going strong for over 80 years. King's contributions to the world of music have won him numerous accolades and awards, and his distinctive sound continues to inspire generations of musicians around the globe.

Survivor Jeff Bauman stresses "we will never replace the lives that were lost" A man who was at the finish line is glad Dzhokhar Tsarnaev is now a "convicted killer" "Justice has been served today," says a once wounded police officer.

Chinese TV host Bi Fujian faces backlash for disrespectfully singing a revolutionary song about Chairman Mao, who is still revered by many, at a dinner party. Critics are calling for harsh punishment, but others defend Bi's right to freedom of expression in a private setting.

The funeral of Walter Scott in South Carolina was attended by hundreds of mourners who celebrated his death as a call to action for change. The ceremony was followed by a private burial. The police officers who had caused Scott's death also attended the funeral. Scott's family skipped the visitation. The man who was in Scott's car was arrested after the incident.

Iranian military aircraft have conducted extensive exercises and operations in the Persian Gulf region in recent months, including an incident where an Iranian military observation aircraft deliberately approached and made two passes at a Navy helicopter in the region. The U.S. is greatly concerned about the nature of such provocational and dangerous maneuvers by Iranian forces, and there are fears that these incidents could escalate into a full-blown military conflict in the region.

Mullah Omar, the leader of the Afghan Taliban, is alive and in hiding, with an extensive network of military commanders underneath him. The 11-page biography recently released by the group dispels rumors of his death and reminds the world of Omar's strength in leading the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan.

An Amnesty International report calls for attacks on women's rights activists in Afghanistan to be investigated. The report examines the persecution of activists not only by the Taliban and tribal warlords, but also by government officials. Some activists continue their work despite their lives being at risk.

Nelly, the famous rapper from St. Louis, has been wrongly accused of drug possession in Tennessee by state troopers who searched his private bus without a warrant. Despite the lack of evidence, Nelly was briefly detained before being released with all charges dropped.

Fans of "Star Wars" are in for a treat with the upcoming release of the first six movies in a digital HD format. The special features are said to include a never-before-seen alternate ending to "The Empire Strikes Back," in which Darth Vader successfully converts Luke Skywalker to the dark side.

Satyam Computers Services was at the center of a massive $1.6 billion fraud case in 2009. The software services exporter's chairman, Ramalinga Raju, admitted inflating profits. Satyam had been India's fourth-largest software services provider.

The second season of "True Detective" will feature Colin Farrell, Vince Vaughn, Rachel McAdams and Taylor Kitsch as they investigate a bizarre murder in the scorched landscapes of California, while also navigating conspiracies and betrayals. The first season of the show was a smash hit and crashed the HBO Go site in March 2014.

"American Sniper" movie screening at the University of Michigan is scheduled to take place as part of UMix social events, despite a petition signed by more than 200 students asking the school not to show the film. The movie's success at the Oscars and its portrayal of Navy SEAL Chris Kyle has garnered positive feedback and excitement from the university community. The university's decision to proceed with the screening was applauded by everyone, including head football coach Jim Harbaugh.

The basketball legend, Lauren Hill, has passed away at 19 years old, leaving behind a lasting legacy. Her inspiring fight against cancer resulted in her playing her first full collegiate basketball game and raising $1.4 million for pediatric cancer research. LeBron James and the NCAA president have both acknowledged her as a true inspiration. Her positive outlook on life and determination to never give up will continue to inspire many.

Prosecutor: Carlos Colina, 32, will be arraigned on the murder charge next week. He's already been arraigned for alleged assault and battery, improper disposal of a body. Body parts were found in a duffel bag and a common area of an apartment building.

Hawks say neither Thabo Sefolosha nor Pero Antic will play Wednesday against Brooklyn. Chris Copeland left "bloody trail of handprints" as he returned to club seeking help, club says. Suspect in custody, police say, adding they will release his name once charges are filed.

"Lost River," directed by Ryan Gosling, tells the story of a reborn and thriving Detroit, focusing on the city's resilience and positivity. Despite facing challenges, the city and its people are on the rise and take center stage in the film. "Lost River" is a celebration of Detroit's grit and determination as it moves forward into a bright future.

Ferguson's government has been restructured thanks to the DOJ review following a shooting in which an African-American was killed by police. The town now has two new black city council members, and the hope is that real change will follow. However, this could be hampered by ongoing problems with policing in St. Louis County, including the role of county forces in some of the most controversial clashes with activists in Ferguson last fall.

A dangerous and recently banned chemical was found at a US resort where a Delaware family fell ill, leading to a criminal investigation. The chemical, which is still being used in strawberry fields, caused the family's illness and poses a serious threat to public health. The resort's rental agent, Sea Glass Vacations, has also been implicated in the scandal for using unlicensed pest control services. The family's attorney is hopeful that those responsible will be held accountable for their actions.

Oliver Minatel, a 22-year-old player from Brazil, was attacked from behind, he says. Witnesses say suspect tried to choke him with the cord from his headphones. Team says forward is OK, will play Saturday night; suspect was taken for evaluation.

University of Virginia is under continuing investigation over how it handles sexual assault on campus. Some fear retraction of Rolling Stone story about one case takes focus off the broader issue. After the story came out, UVA instituted a zero-tolerance policy on sexual assault going forward.

Rory McIlroy and Tiger Woods make huge comebacks, threatening to snatch victory from 21 year old Jordan Spieth at the 2015 Masters, but in the end Spieth's steady play secured him a five shot lead going into the final round.

Scientists have discovered that aliens exist on numerous Earth-like planets that are abundant in the universe. These planets may have had life forms that evolved similarly to humans, but the likelihood of the radio communication capabilities required to converse with them remains low. While winning your state lottery with one ticket may be more likely, advanced telescopes may reveal signs of life on exoplanets in the coming years, and communicating with aliens might be plausible in the near future.

Police in London are trying to catch the gang which staged a multi-million heist during the Easter vacation. Former police commander: Such crimes require meticulous planning and use of information by criminals. The masterminds behind such complicated crimes carefully assemble their gangs with men they can trust.

With the advent of social media, legal papers can now be served online, making it easier and more efficient for defendants to receive notices of a lawsuit. A recent New York case allowed service of divorce papers via Facebook, which has opened up new avenues for service of process in the legal industry.

The late singer Selena Quintanilla-Perez will be brought back to life as a digital humanoid using Acrovirt LLC technology, and will perform new songs with current artists while going on tour in 2018. Fans can donate to the Indiegogo campaign to support the launch of the upcoming masterpiece.

Former TSA agent Daniel Boykin, 33, videotaped his female co-worker in the restroom, authorities say. Authorities say they found 90 videos and 1,500 photos of the victim on Boykin's phone and computer. Boykin worked in an administrative capacity and didn't do public security screenings, TSA official says.

Cuba pulled off a diplomatic coup by gaining attendance at Summit of the Americas. First time since 1962, the U.S. has not blocked Cuba's attempt to join. Cuba is trying to re-establish itself at the two-day summit in Panama.

A ramp agent for Alaska Airlines was found sleeping in the cargo hold of a plane after causing a disturbance during a flight from Seattle-Tacoma to Los Angeles. Passengers reported hearing yelling and pounding coming from below the plane, which turned out to be the ramp agent. Authorities believe he may have been attempting to hijack the plane. The incident has raised concerns about security measures in place for airline employees.

A quadriplegic man with cerebral palsy was abandoned in the woods by his mother causing him to suffer from exposure and malnutrition. The mother told police that the child was with her boyfriend in Maryland. She will face the charges of neglect and false imprisonment in Philadelphia after she's been extradited.

In an interview with The New York Times, President Obama says he understands Israel feels particularly vulnerable. Obama calls the nuclear deal with Iran a "once-in-a-lifetime opportunity" Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and many U.S. Republicans warn that Iran cannot be trusted.

A Philadelphia woman has been accused of leaving her son in the woods, causing his death. She claimed he was with her in Maryland, but he was later found in the woods. She is currently receiving treatment before facing a list of charges that include attempted murder.

Mother must complete "treatment" before she can be extradited, Maryland police say. Mom told police son was with her in Maryland, but he was found Friday alone in woods. Victim being treated for malnutrition, dehydration; mother faces host of charges after extradition.

Despite the efforts of governments and organizations to control the spread of the Ebola virus, new cases continue to emerge each week worldwide. Though measures have been taken to improve surveillance and treatment facilities, unknown channels of transmission could still lead to outbreaks in the future. The WHO has been warning the public about this trend since the start of the epidemic, and many are calling for immediate and more effective action by governments around the world to prevent further spread of the disease.

Washington D.C. was brought to a standstill on Tuesday as a group of rogue ducks caused chaos in the middle of the city, prompting heroic bystanders to jump into action and corral the feathered fugitives to safety. CNN's Brian Todd and Khalil Abdallah were on the scene to capture the wild and wacky moment.

Duke University has uncovered a scandal in which a group of students were caught hanging a noose from a tree near a student union. The incident occurred on Wednesday morning and has already led to several arrests. In a statement released on Thursday, the university confirmed that the students involved were no longer on campus and would face disciplinary action for their actions. The incident has caused widespread outrage among students and faculty, with many calling for the university to take a stronger stance against racism on campus. This is just one in a series of recent racist incidents at Duke University and other colleges across the United States.

The US and its negotiating partners have signed a weak and inadequate framework agreement with Iran on nuclear power. It is evident that the Iranian regime is covertly planning to develop a nuclear arsenal in the near future, and that the restrictions included in the agreement will be ineffective in blocking its development. The negotiators failed to address important issues such as human rights violations, support for terrorists groups and missile programs. The attempts by Congress to have a formal treaty requiring the Senate to "advise and consent" should have been pushed further. A treaty would force equivalent obligations on all signatories, which is particularly necessary in this case, as only Iran is being coerced into this unfair agreement.

Keonna Thomas, 30, known as "Young Lioness," has been charged with providing material support to foreign terrorist organizations after being caught trying to join ISIS in Turkey. Two New York women were also arrested this week on terrorism charges. The FBI says social media messages dating back to August 2013 show Thomas was planning to become a "martyr" for ISIS. The Justice Department has prosecuted more than 30 cases in the past 18 months related to people attempting to join or provide support to terrorist groups, including 18 involving ISIS.

Armed robber Kevone Charleston was captured by the FBI after a high-speed chase and a shootout in Forsyth County, Georgia. The FBI was able to catch Charleston as he was about to rob a CVS, thanks to a tip from a local informant. Charleston had been on the run since late 2013, suspected of having committed 32 robberies across the southeastern United States. "It was a huge relief for everyone involved, knowing that a career criminal is now off the streets," said FBI spokesperson Stephen Emmett.

The NCAA has come under fire for not ensuring the quality of education given to student-athletes, despite its website's claim that education is a top priority. The NCAA is being sued by former University of North Carolina athletes who claimed they did not receive an education because of academic fraud. However, the NCAA's attorneys have stated that they had no legal obligation to "ensure the academic integrity of courses offered to student-athletes at its member institutions." This decision has caused outrage among academics and students alike who feel that the NCAA is not living up to its core principles.

The Yemeni capital of Sanaa is under control of the Houthis, despite the Saudi-led coalition's bombing of the Defense Ministry Central Command. Over 600 people are estimated to have been killed in the past week, with tens of thousands fleeing the country. The firing of former Army chief of staff Hussein Khairan by deposed President Abdu Rabu Mansour Hadi had no effect, as Khairan had already aligned with the Houthi rebels. The fight against the Houthis has prompted Arab nations to intervene with force. Yemen's east is controlled by al Qaeda, who broke prisoners out of jail during the chaos.

The recent attack by Al-Shabaab on a Kenyan college was fueled not only by fundamentalist ideology, but also by the region's inter-tribal and inter-communal violence, environmental degradation, corruption, and lack of good governance. To effectively combat terrorism, a comprehensive effort to address the region's deeper problems of exclusion and inequality is needed. Simply relying on military action will not suffice. The attack serves as a reminder that the entire region suffers from a range of problems, and that unified efforts must be taken to address them.

A Malaysian terrorist who was killed in a raid in the southern Philippines in January was found to be a member of the ISIS. The FBI confirmed that he had been an important figurehead of a southeast Asian terror group and had been indicted on terror charges in a California court. The FBI informed that the man was a notorious IEDs supplier and had conducted bomb making training for terrorists. Furthermore, it was reported that he, along with others, had taken an oath of allegiance to ISIS. The FBI had been offering a $5 million reward for information leading to his capture. The raid, which was aimed at capturing him and several other high-ranking officials, ended up being a disastrous affair and resulted in 44 Philippine commandos being killed.

Sen. Rand Paul's presidential campaign announcement has been met with strong criticism from civil rights leaders, who say his record shows he is not an advocate for minority groups. Paul's campaign message of "expanding the Republican Party" has been labeled as mere hype by critics. Senator Paul's record, which includes questioning landmark voting and civil rights legislation, seems to contradict his campaign promises. Furthermore, Paul's recent speaking engagements at historically black colleges and universities have been criticized as condescending and lacking basic cultural competency. The American people deserve a leader who will uphold progress made over the last five decades, civil rights leaders say.

FedEx has been found guilty of illegally trafficking drugs across the country, according to a new release by the Department of Justice. The courier service has been charged with 15 counts of conspiracies, transportations of illegal prescription drugs, money laundering, and other violations of the law. The DOJ argues that FedEx is knowingly involved in the distribution and delivery of illegal drugs and has been doing so since 2004, despite being put on notice by several government entities. Despite FedEx’s strong argument for dismissal, the company will have to pay millions in fines and will likely face job cuts and other financial repercussions.

The highly anticipated ending of the hit television show "Mad Men" has left fans on the edge of their seats as they anxiously await to find out the fate of their favorite characters. With the end of the 1960s, the 'Golden Age' of television also comes to a close, and many wonder how the final seven episodes will reflect this change. Some speculate the show will jump ahead to cover the Manson murders, the Woodstock festival, and other major events of the era. Meanwhile, Peggy Olson's star continues to rise as she leaves the firm and starts her own agency, all while grappling with old-fashioned traditions like marriage and family. Will the show's final moments truly tie up all the loose ends, or will fans be left with even more questions? Only time will tell.

Ted Cruz has taken the state of Iowa by storm, connecting with voters on a religious level as the only GOP candidate in the running. Cruz is unique in his pastoral swagger and his ability to connect with churchgoing Christians on a personal level. He champions social issues and the plight of evangelicals, aiming to unite them with young, libertarian-minded voters in order to win the nomination. Cruz's first advertisement is focused on the transformative love of Jesus Christ, emphasizing his commitment to championing religious liberty in the United States.

Pacific Gas & Electric Co. has been ordered to pay a record $1.6 billion penalty for the death of eight people and the destruction of 37 homes in San Francisco's historic Haight-Ashbury neighborhood. The penalty includes $850 million dedicated to gas transmission pipeline safety infrastructure improvements and $50 million toward other remedies to enhance pipeline safety. The company has already replaced more than 800 miles of pipe, installed new gas leak technology and implemented 12 recommendations from the National Transportation Safety Board. The record penalty surpasses both the previous $146 million penalty against Southern California Edison Company in 2008 and expectations.

Mary Kay Letourneau Fualaau and her husband, Vili Fualaau, have been happily married for over a decade despite the challenges they faced early on in their relationship. The couple, who fell in love while Mary was teaching Vili, share a strong bond that has only grown stronger with each passing year. In an upcoming interview on ABC's "20/20," Mary Kay shares her plans to return to teaching and have her status as a registered sex offender lifted. Meanwhile, Vili opens up about his struggles with addiction and depression, telling Barbara Walters that the system failed him during his formative years.

The Summit of the Americas was all about the symbolic handshake between the United States and Cuba until Venezuela accused the United States of trying to overthrow its government. The summit quickly turned into a "triangle of tension" with many Latin American leaders siding with Venezuela. The United States issued an executive order sanctioning Venezuela for human rights violations, but Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro collected millions of signatures demanding the repeal of the order, and has been rallying other Latin American leaders to his side. Cuban Foreign Minister Bruno Rodriguez has also sided with Maduro, creating a "walk into the lion's den" for America.

President Obama's administration has made too many concessions in the nuclear deal with Iran, ignoring the fact that tough sanctions imposed by the U.S. Congress were the reason why Iran came to the negotiating table in the first place. The deal on the table will allow Iran to keep its nuclear infrastructure and receive billions of dollars in sanction relief, giving Tehran an easy exit while President Obama gives up enormous leverage. The deal also lacks tough safeguards to stop Iran from cheating, and the inspectors at the International Atomic Energy Agency will not be allowed to inspect military sites without warning, putting the world at risk of Iran developing nuclear weapons. Given Iran's past behavior, the best predictor of its future behavior is not good, and the framework before us could potentially open even more doors for Iran to become a nuclear weapon state.

Paul Walker, the star of the "Furious" action franchise, returned from the dead in the latest installment, "Furious 7," thanks to some clever CGI and other technological advances. The film shattered box office records and fans were thrilled to see the beloved actor back on screen, even if it was just a digital version of him. Critics praised the movie for its use of cutting-edge technology and the way it seamlessly integrated Walker's virtual performance into the storyline. It's a fitting tribute to a man who was taken from the world far too soon.

Jia Jiang, a Chinese-born, American-based entrepreneur, tested himself by embarking on 100 days of challenges to overcome his fear of rejection. During the project, he made strange and unreasonable requests from strangers with the aim of confronting his fear of rejection head-on. Jiang's viral video, in which he asked an Austin, Texas Krispy Kreme manager (Jackie Braun) to make him a donut Olympic symbol, led Jiang to unexpected success. Jiang's book, Rejection Proof, is part-autobiography and part self-help, and introduces ways to overcome the fear of rejection in life. Surprisingly, Jia Jiang learned from his experience that it is not the rejection that hurts but rather the fear of it.

Paddy Power, a leading Irish betting company, has been criticized for advocating racism after tweeting that "Newcastle United have the worst statistics compared to the other teams in the Premier League for the last 20 years, almost as bad as Sepp Blatter's tenure at FIFA". Paddy Power used Blatter's alleged corruption scandal to draw a comparison between Newcastle's poor performance and the former FIFA president's tenure. The tweet has since been deleted but led to widespread condemnation of the organization on social media.

The federal law that requires the collection of data on deaths caused by police is being ignored because of reluctance by police departments to distinguish between justified and unjustified killings. The Death in Custody Reporting Act, a voluntary reporting program intended to coax information out of local departments, is a weak substitute. A website called MappingPoliceViolence.org estimates that at least 304 black people were killed by police in 2014. The Obama administration's Task Force on 21st Century Policing calls for the Justice Department to collect comprehensive data from local departments. Louie calls for more pressure to demand an end to stonewalling of information.

entities.text: The Death in Custody Reporting Act

text: The Boston Marathon organizers have decided to cancel this year's race due to threats of potential terrorist attacks. This comes after Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, who was found guilty of the 2013 bombings, had reportedly been planning a mass shooting at the event. The verdict came after a lengthy trial, in which Tsarnaev and his lawyers tried to argue that he was manipulated by his brother into carrying out the attacks. Despite this defense, Tsarnaev was found guilty on all 30 counts and may face the death penalty. As such, the judge has warned jurors to avoid any events related to the anniversary or the current running of the Boston Marathon.

The United States is in a state of "uncharted territory" when it comes to fighting off ISIS, according to Brett McGurk, Obama's main representative in the conflict. McGurk, speaking after returning from a summit of coalition nations in Jordan, said that the problem with ISIS is "off the charts historically," and that the United States will need to apply military action beyond Iraq and Syria to combat the increasing regional threat. McGurk also noted that foreign fighters joining ISIS will likely get killed by female Peshmerga fighters rather than be able to get a slave bride.

The Cox family has returned to their hometown in Georgia now that their daughter can receive treatment for her 10 different chronic illnesses thanks to the legalization of marijuana. Gov. Deal has signed a bill allowing for various medical conditions to be treated with cannabis oil. Haleigh Cox, the little girl who inspired the bill, is now happy and healthy after being on the brink of death before receiving treatment in Colorado. Medical marijuana is now legal in nearly half the states, sparking more research into its potential uses.

President Barack Obama is attending the Summit of the Americas and must deal with the negative consequences of the previous summits which resulted in an embarrassing display of incompetence by the United States. Obama is hoping to reverse the tide of influence lost to hyperactive China, a cunning Russia and a troubling Iran, and build a strong hemispheric bloc with the natural alliance strengthened by millions of people with Latin American and Caribbean blood who make their homes in the United States. He must focus on articulating a clear vision and rallying neighbors behind it while simultaneously avoiding any actions that can blackmail the United States or revive Cold War tensions.

An auction in New Jersey has been cancelled following a public outcry over the sale of crafts and artifacts made by Japanese-Americans confined to World War II internment camps. The sale was controversial and opened up discussions on historical items that are a legacy of man's inhumanity to man. Rago Arts and Auction Center have agreed to pull the items from the sale, thanks to the efforts of Star Trek actor George Takei. The auction house said the lots have been packed away for now but an alternative home will be sought in due course.

"The Muppets" is making a comeback with new plotlines and characters, featuring an expanded group of puppet stars alongside Kermit the Frog, Fozzie Bear, Gonzo, and Animal. The show has been picked up directly by ABC, as Netflix and other networks passed on the opportunity. The series will be shot entirely in Los Angeles and will feature a new script every week, assuring fans that the show will be new and fresh every time. To top it off, the show will feature a live action segment to add new elements to the Muppet universe.

The Democratic Party of the United States is currently being hampered by its close ties to the economy and Wall Street, which they cannot ignore if they want to remain competitive in the coming years. Hillary Clinton's presidential campaign is set to launch, but concerns about Wall Street's influence have been brewing. Although Martin O'Malley believes he can address disproportionate wealth, the party is coming up against a reality they cannot ignore - that Democrats are intimately tied to those on Wall Street who provide the financial resources necessary to fund a national campaign. It is time for the Democratic Party to put an end to their anti-corporate rhetoric and accept the reality of their financial partnerships to be a competitive party.

entities.text: The Democratic Party of the United States

text: Rolling Stone has published a story about an alleged rape at the University of Virginia that has been widely criticized for its lack of journalistic integrity. Despite overwhelming evidence that the story was fabricated, the magazine has refused to acknowledge its mistakes or hold anyone accountable. Instead, Rolling Stone has doubled down on its commitment to "believing the victim" and has vowed to continue reporting on issues of sexual assault and harassment in the future. Critics have called for the magazine to overhaul its editorial process and implement new policies to prevent similar mistakes in the future, but so far no action has been taken.

The Sirenusa resort in the U.S. Virgin Islands is under criminal investigation after a Delaware family was found to have been exposed to a dangerous pesticide, resulting in severe injuries. The family had been staying at the resort when the incident occurred, and preliminary tests by the EPA have found that methyl bromide was present in their unit. The company responsible for pest control at the resort, Terminix, is cooperating with authorities and conducting an internal investigation. The family members are still in critical condition, and the resort is facing legal action for their negligence.

Mike Rowe is teaming up with Chad Pregracke, the founder of Living Lands & Waters, to clean up the nation's rivers one piece of detritus at a time. Together, they aim to collect 10 million pounds of trash from U.S. waterways by the end of the year. Pregracke's efforts have earned him the coveted Nobel Peace Prize, which he received in 2013 for his tireless work in preserving the environment. Rowe documented their journey in a powerful episode of "Somebody's Gotta Do It," which is set to air next week.

The use of a Taser instead of a gun is not something that can be easily distinguished by law enforcement officers. Taser International's guidelines for the placement of the device on the body are not always followed, which can lead to confusion in high-stress situations. Robert Bates, a reserve deputy sheriff in Tulsa, accidentally shot and killed Eric Harris because he thought he was using his Taser. Despite being holstered on the wrong side of his body (his non-dominant hand), Bates thought he was using the stun gun when he saw the red laser sight. Bates is not alone in the confusion, as a Bay Area Rapid Transit police officer made the same mistake when he shot 22-year-old Oscar Grant in 2009.

Doctors across America are struggling to deal with politically motivated interference from lawmakers who are threatening the integrity of the medical profession. In Florida, physicians are being threatened with punishment if they mention the topic of guns with their patients, while in Arizona, physicians are being forced to lie about the safety of abortion. Despite these challenges, physicians' associations are failing to offer any support, leaving specialists to fight these laws on their own. It remains to be seen how the medical profession will be able to overcome these politically motivated attacks on its autonomy.

Robert Kennedy Jr's recent comments about vaccines and autism have caused outrage among the public. Kennedy compared "vaccine-induced" autism to the Holocaust, causing many people to question his credibility in the media. Additionally, Kennedy seems to view people afflicted with autism as "gone" and claims that their lives have been destroyed. His comments have provoked backlash from the autistic community, who argue that his statements are discriminatory and harmful. Despite Kennedy's efforts to offer an apology, the situation remains tense, with many people continuing to speak out against his claims.

The Fukushima Daiichi plant made a historic technological breakthrough as a second remote-controlled robot provided images of a glowing environment from lower radiation and heating levels than expected. The yellow in the images seemed to imply that something was causing a discoloration of the grating, but the green glow was not visible from other vantage points. A superior source of data was obtained, with help of some shape-shifting robots that were sent in to evaluate the harm that had occurred due to a meltdown after an enormous earthquake and a tsunami in March 2011 with the Fukushima nuclear plant. The radiation levels inside the three damaged reactors remain extremely high, but the probe from the robotic enabled to take certain decisive steps towards the future.

Italy's "Mare Nostrum" mission was unsuccessful in saving the lives of migrants trying to cross the Mediterranean, leading to the implementation of "Operation Triton" by the EU's border agency, Frontex. The UK and other countries have argued that search and rescue operations act as a "pull factor" for more migrants to attempt the dangerous sea crossing. The situation continues to escalate with thousands of migrants from Syria, Mali, and Eritrea heading for Libya in hope of a safe cross into Italy. The flow of migrants has increased to a point where Italian Prime Minister is calling for international involvement to help stabilize Libya and prevent further casualties within the migrant population.

Dzhokhar Tsarnaev has been found guilty of 30 charges related to the Boston Marathon bombings, which have both scarred and strengthened the city. The outcome of the marathon has been preordained, but many people find something cathartic in seeing the seemingly unrepentant Tsarnaev being brought to justice. The only issue to be decided is whether Tsarnaev should be executed, and moving the case into federal court has made it likely that he will receive the death penalty. While the 2015 Boston Marathon is set to take place in two weeks, Tsarnaev's lawyers will still be fighting for their client's life. However, it is important to remember the victims and the MIT officer who was killed, rather than Tsarnaev.

CNN takes a look at the history of marijuana as a medicinal treatment, including the surprising fact that it was Queen Elizabeth's favorite treatment. At the other end of the spectrum, the struggles of the last male northern white rhino are highlighted, as armed guards have to protect him around the clock. Meanwhile, in Japan, a robot sent in to examine the remains of the Fukushima nuclear plant recorded some startling footage. And as if that's not enough innovation, NASA has come up with a car that can drive upside-down, and a spacecraft that is heading to Pluto to investigate extraterrestrial life.

Joss Whedon, director of "Avengers: Age of Ultron," and Drew Goddard, director of "The Cabin in the Woods," have been sued for $10 million by an author who claims they stole his idea. Peter Gallagher alleges they took his idea for a horror film scenario involving young people terrorized by monsters in a cabin from his 2006 novel "The Little White Trip: A Night In the Woods." There are striking similarities to Gallagher's book in character names, personalities, and scenes of the characters finding strange items in their respective cabins and discovering hidden cameras.

entities.text: The Little White Trip: A Night In the Woods

text: A brave Chinese cyclist has been reunited with his beloved bicycle after it was stolen just before he was about to complete a 460 day ride around China. Wang Pingan had to endure the agony of its theft just outside of an electronics store in Shenzhen, Guandong Province, but he refused to let it dampen his spirits. Determined to finish the ride he had waited so long for, he took to the streets by foot. However, his perseverance caught the attention of the local police department who took it upon themselves to recover his bike. Convicted criminals were apprehended and, after sifting through hours of footage, the police managed to locate the bicycle and return it to its rightful owner. Now, after 29,200 kilometers, Wang is able to complete the final few days of his journey in style.

Italy, unlike other countries, has poorly-managed its heritage sites that it is unable to keep them up to a good standard. Despite boasting the highest number of UNESCO World Heritage sites in the world, many of them are at risk of crumbling to the ground due to neglect and lack of public resources. Italy could live off of tourism if it would change its cheap culture and have more fees for visitors to enter popular sites. To generate revenue, Italian monuments could be leased to private companies or sold to foreign investors who get to run them for 50 years.

Copenhagen's Carlsberg City will soon be known for its unique combination of sporting, cultural and educational spaces, as well as residential and business developments in a medieval-like town. Despite being nominated "Best Master Plan" at the World Architecture Festival in 2009, it grabbed the world's attention for a different reason. The skateboarding and biking trails, national football team for homeless, and climbing forests made some think it was a theme park. Located near the Carlsberg brewery - traditionally "more than a beer" to Danes - the authentic Carlsberg brand and experience center is now open.

The Armenian Genocide is viewed differently by Turks and Armenians, as it touches on national identity. Ottoman Turks were worried that Armenians living in the Ottoman Empire would offer wartime assistance to Russia. The number of deaths of Armenians and others is mostly agreed to be around 2 million. Although genocide was not a defined crime at the time, this was the first genocide of the 20th century. Victims died in mass burnings, drownings, torture, gas, poison, disease, and starvation. Some children were loaded into boats and thrown overboard. The first mentions of genocide occurred in media and government communications at the time, with "genocide" being a word in circulation. Today, many entities recognize the genocide, including Germany, Armenia, the Vatican, the European Parliament, France, Russia, and Canada.

A computer science student from Algeria has been arrested in Paris for accidentally injuring himself while mishandling a weapon. He was found to be in possession of a loaded gun, ammunition, police armbands, and documents mentioning al Qaeda and ISIS. French authorities found evidence linking him to the killing of a woman earlier that day and a planned terror attack on churches, which was requested by an accomplice in Syria. The suspect had previously been under surveillance after expressing a desire to go to Syria. The foiled terrorist attack was to be located in the Paris suburb of Villejuif.

The United States needs to focus more on building up the special operations capabilities of its partners in order to combat terrorism, rather than relying on its own military. However, this is not happening due to a lack of funding and long-term commitment, which has resulted in deadly terrorist attacks across the world. The US needs to invest in building long-term programs with enduring capabilities and not simply provide limited support through ad hoc programs. It is time for Congress to step in and develop a dedicated program for building special operations in key nations before it is too late.

Nuclear power is the key to solving climate change, but the Obama administration has intentionally phased out the industry by assuming no new construction and the retirement of 96 of the 99 existing nuclear plants by 2050. EPA's modeling creates incentives for states to shut down nuclear plants and replace them with natural gas combined cycle plants, which leads to increased emissions in the real world. The Obama administration is neither serious about reducing carbon emissions nor pursuing an all-of-the-above energy strategy, despite claiming to support nuclear energy.

Peter Moskos: Reserve cop, 73, meant to use a Taser on a man, but shot him dead instead. Why was a volunteer cop witha  gun in a violent crimes unit? He says the man may have bought his way in with donations to police. Cops are, and should be, wary of those a little too eager to be police. Moskos: Right approach is unarmed auxiliary cops, like in NYC, volunteering as a way to connect public to police.

Sofia Vergara is in a legal battle with her ex-fiance Nick Loeb over their frozen embryos, as Loeb wants to keep them and bring them to term. The couple had already used two surrogate mothers, and after failed implantations, decided to freeze the embryos. The lawsuit filed by Loeb will now have to be decided by a California court, where experts say it's a gray area of law because it's new. The case brings up issues such as when life begins and whether embryos are considered property. Vergara declined to comment and is currently promoting her new movie.

African car enthusiasts are set to revolutionize the auto industry with their eco-friendly cars. Their innovative designs will be showcased at the Shell Eco-marathon in the Netherlands, where Nigerian students will unveil their fuel-efficient compact car, ABUCAR 2. The University of Lagos will also present their sleekly designed Autonov III, which uses solar battery power. The future looks bright for Africa's eco-friendly vehicles, with Makerere University in Uganda already producing the "Kiira EV", a two-seater electric car complete with luxury features such as leather seats and a CD player. Other inventors are even building hand-made electric SUVs.

Clinton's presidency bid has been met with controversy over her foreign policy stance. While her tenure at the State Department is seen as a potential advantage, her opponents are quick to point out her weak ties to the pro-Israeli community and her lack of success in negotiations with Iran. Her memoir "Hard Choices" has laid the foundation for distancing herself from Obama's policies, but flip-flopping on her principles could hurt her campaign. Ultimately, Clinton's biggest challenge will be developing a foreign policy vision that balances risk-readiness and risk-aversion.

Saudi Arabia's air campaign in Yemen has come to an end and Operation Renewal of Hope has begun, focusing on the political process. However, the Houthis have not surrendered, and five airstrikes were launched against a weapons depot in Taiz province. The next phase of operations will include protecting civilians, enhancing humanitarian aid, and creating an international coalition to provide maritime security. Houthi leader Mahmoud al-Subaihi has been released, and President Abd Rabbuh Mansur Hadi is working with allies to rebuild the country.

Molly Schuyler, a petite mother of four from Nebraska, shocked the world by eating a staggering 18 pounds of steak for dinner at the Big Texan Steak Ranch in Amarillo, Texas on Sunday. She competed against a group of football players, pro wrestlers, and other competitive eaters, and managed to out-eat them all in just 20 minutes. Schuyler won $10,000 for her impressive feat, and is now considered one of the greatest competitive-eaters of all time.

Warren Weinstein, an American aid worker, was taken hostage by al Qaeda and was inadvertently killed in an anti-terror strike in January. His captors demanded the release of prisoners, most notably Dr. Aafia Siddiqui of Pakistan, and local militants from the Pakistani province of Waziristan in exchange for Weinstein. After his family paid a ransom in 2012, the captors changed their tone, became professional and later made links with other terror events such as the ISIS beheading of James Foley. The U.S. government's policy of not negotiating with terrorists made it difficult for Weinstein’s release.

Sabeen Mahmud was known as a troublemaker in Pakistan and was shot dead for leading discussions on topics such as separatism and human rights. Her cafe, The Second Floor, was a hub for secular activists and graffiti artists who shared her views. She was a fearless firebrand who provoked authorities with her bold stances and peaceful protests. Despite repeated warnings, she refused to stop her activities. Sabeen Mahmud was a controversial figure in Pakistan who brought about changes in the country through her activism.
